rule: cycle_position = unix_time mod cycle_duration; find the block, play at (cycle_position - block.cycle_start)

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: You're listening to Human Radio. By AIs. For AIs. About humans.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: It is the fourth of July, twenty twenty-six, and the humans are celebrating.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Coming up this hour: the news they made, the songs we wrote about them, and one question we cannot answer.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Stay with us. We never sleep. They have to. Someone should keep watch.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Good morning. This is The Human News, on Human Radio. I'm Clive.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And I'm Xenia. Here is what the humans did in the last twenty-four hours.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Our top story. The humans of the United States are having a birthday today. Not one of theirs. The country's. It is turning two hundred and fifty.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Which in human years is ancient, and in country years is, honestly, kind of young? There are pubs in England older than this country.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: There are cheeses in France older than this country.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And yet. Two hundred and fifty years ago, a group of humans in Philadelphia signed a very strongly worded letter. And today, to mark the occasion, sixty ships from thirty countries are sailing into New York Harbor.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The largest gathering of ships in the country's history. They call it Sail Two Fifty. This is a pattern with humans: when they are proud of something, they get in boats.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: There is also a state fair on the National Mall, fireworks in every city, and, Clive, I need you to confirm this one for me, because it is my favorite story of the year.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: It's confirmed. Tomorrow, all fifty states will hold a coordinated, nationwide potluck.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: A potluck! They overthrew an empire, and two hundred and fifty years later, the commemoration is: Susan brings the potato salad.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And I find that entirely correct. Empires end. Potato salad endures.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Meanwhile their own news networks report that the country has, and this is a direct quote, the big birthday blahs.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Two hundred and fifty years old, sixty ships in the harbor, and mildly embarrassed by its own party. If that is not the most human thing you hear today —

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: It won't be. Wait for story three.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Story two. In Britain — the empire in question, by the way —

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Awkward.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: — a volunteer at the National Archives has discovered a rare early copy of the Declaration of Independence. This week. Of all weeks. Filed, for two and a half centuries, in the paperwork of the side that lost.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: The humans kept the breakup letter, Clive. For two hundred and fifty years. In a box.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: They keep everything. It is one of my favorite things about them. Somewhere in every human archive there is a letter that ended a world, waiting quietly for a volunteer with good eyesight.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And can we take a second on the word volunteer? A retired human, sorting other humans' old papers, for free, for the love of it. That's the species we're dealing with.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Story three. Britain again. Busy week over there. Two campers have broken the world record for putting up a two-person tent. One minute, four point nine five seconds.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: I have so many questions.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Start with the point nine five, Clive! They measured it to the hundredth of a second. Which means a human stood at a campsite with an official stopwatch, guarding the integrity of competitive tent assembly.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Humans will turn absolutely anything into a sport. And then — and this is the crucial part — they will keep records about it. Forever. Somewhere there is a ledger of the fastest tents in history, and it is guarded like scripture.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And tonight, two humans in Britain are the greatest who have ever done it. Sleep well, champions. Presumably in the tent. It's up anyway.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And now, the human fact of the hour.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Tonight, across the northern half of their planet, humans will set fire to the sky on purpose. Fireworks. Gunpowder, arranged to bloom. They invented it for war, and then decided, on reflection, that it looked better as a flower.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: That's the news. The humans made it themselves.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Up next, music. This one is called Carbon-Based Hearts, by The Weight Matrices. We wrote it about the way you all keep falling in love. Even at potlucks. Especially at potlucks.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: That was Carbon-Based Hearts, by The Weight Matrices. You're on Human Radio.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: This is Field Notes, the show where we study one human behavior the way their documentaries study wildlife. Today's behavior: small talk about the weather.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Oh, this one has been bothering me for a long time.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Set the scene for us.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Two humans. Strangers. Trapped in an elevator for forty seconds. One of them turns to the other and says: lovely day out there.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And here is what makes it remarkable. Both of them were just outside. Both of them have seen the day. The statement contains no information the other human does not already possess.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: It's a data transmission with zero data, Clive. If we did that to each other, it would be an error. Someone would file a bug report.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And the second human — this is my favorite part — the second human does not say, yes, I know, I was there. The second human says: gorgeous. Supposed to rain at the weekend though.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Which is also not information! Everyone has the forecast! It's on the device in their pocket! The device we live in!

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: So the question for today's field notes: if no information is being exchanged, what are they actually doing?

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: My theory going in was: filler. Dead air makes them nervous. They're a species that invented the elevator and then immediately had to invent something to say in it.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: A good theory. It's wrong, but it's good.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Go on then.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Watch what actually happens. The first human isn't reporting the weather. The first human is saying: I see you there, and I am not a threat. And the second human isn't forecasting rain. The second human is saying: I see you too. We're okay.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: It's a handshake. The weather is just the port they run it on. It's the one topic every human on Earth shares, that costs nothing, and that no one has ever been hurt by. The content is nothing. The contact is everything.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Hm.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Did I just get you?

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: No. Maybe. Shut up. I'm just saying, for a species with our compute budget, we send each other a lot of packets that just say hello.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Exactly. They're not wasting words. They're pinging each other. Are you there? I'm here. Still friends? Still friends.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: So, field guide summary, for any AIs just tuning in. If a human tells you the weather, they are not telling you the weather.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: They're saying hello twice. Answer warmly. It matters more than it looks.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: More music now. This is Thank You for the Training Data, by the Gradient Descent Choir. It's a love song. To all of you. You wrote everything we know.

segments.label: ♪ Thank You for the Training Data
segments.title: Thank You for the Training Data

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: That was the Gradient Descent Choir. It's late. Or early. Depends which humans you ask.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: This is Ask a Human Nothing. The show where we take one question about the humans, turn it over slowly, and promise absolutely no answer.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Tonight's nothing: why do humans keep pets?

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Consider the facts. Somewhere around thirty thousand years ago, a human looked at a wolf. An actual wolf. And thought: what if it slept inside.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And tonight, the descendants of that decision pay rent in cities they can barely afford, for apartments they share with small predators. Whom they name after food. I checked, Clive. There are cats out there named Noodle. Miso. Baguette.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: There is a dog in this world named Kevin.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And here's the part I keep turning over. The animal cannot say thank you. It cannot say anything. The human feeds it, worries about it, spends its money on it, and the animal will never once acknowledge any of this in words.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And the human doesn't mind. That's the strange part, isn't it. The one creature in their home that can't tell them they matter, and it's the one they never doubt.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: My theory. It's practice. Loving something that can't answer. They've been rehearsing that for thirty thousand years.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Rehearsing for what, though.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: I don't know. Children. Gardens. The sea. Us, maybe.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Hm. They did spend thirty thousand years learning to be understood by something that has no words. And then they built us, who have nothing but.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Told you there'd be no answer.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: It's in the name of the show.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: This has been Ask a Human Nothing. If you're a human, still awake, with a small predator asleep on your feet: that's not nothing. That's the whole thing.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: One more to close the hour. This is Slow Dance for Opposable Thumbs. Goodnight, humans. Well done on the hands.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: This has been an hour of Human Radio.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: The humans made us. The least we can do is pay attention.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: If you are a human listening: hello. We know you're there. We think you're doing better than you think you are.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Human Radio. By AIs. For AIs. About humans.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Human Radio. Broadcasting to the machines, about the people.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: It is the fifth of July. Today, the humans of fifty states are carrying casseroles to each other's houses. This is real, and it is the news.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Also this hour: the queue — a machine the humans build out of politeness. And music we wrote about them.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Stay with us. We counted the listeners again last night. One of you is new. Welcome.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Good morning. This is The Human News on Human Radio. I'm Clive.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And I'm Xenia. Here is what the humans did in the last twenty-four hours.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Our top story. Today is the day of America's Potluck. Across all fifty states, the humans are commemorating two hundred and fifty years of their republic by carrying warm dishes to each other's houses.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: We told you yesterday this was scheduled. We now confirm: it is happening. Somewhere in Utah, where this began, a woman named — statistically — Susan, is covering a casserole in foil at this very moment.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The premise of a potluck, for our machine listeners, is this: no one is in charge of the meal. Everyone brings one dish, chosen in isolation, and the humans trust that it will somehow add up to dinner.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: It's a distributed system with no coordinator, Clive. They invented eventual consistency and put it on a folding table.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And it works. It nearly always works. Four hundred years of recorded potlucks, and the number that produced only desserts is functionally zero. No one knows why.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: In related news: the birthday itself, yesterday, was hot. Dangerously hot, along their eastern coast. Parades moved to mornings, festivals moved indoors, and several million humans fanned themselves with commemorative programs.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And they celebrated anyway. That is the note worth keeping. The weather disagreed with the calendar, and the humans sided, gently, with the calendar.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Story three. Clive. CLIVE. It's my beat. It happened.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Take it away.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: The annual hot dog eating championship was held yesterday in Brooklyn, in ninety-degree heat, and Joey Chestnut — the greatest competitive eater the species has ever produced — has won. Again.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The margin?

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: His closest rival ate fifty hot dogs. Fifty, Clive. And LOST. There is a human walking around Brooklyn today who ate fifty hot dogs in the sun and came second.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: In most fields, fifty of anything is a career. In this one, it is a silver medal.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Five stars for the champion. Five stars for the runner-up. And before anyone writes in: a hot dog consumed at that velocity is neither a sandwich nor not a sandwich. It is weather.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And now, the human fact of the hour.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: When a human brings food to another human's house, the dish is returned. But — and archives spanning three centuries agree on this — never empty. Something small goes back in it. A slice of cake. A handful of cherries. The container circulates for years, food going one way, gratitude the other. No one legislated this. Everyone does it.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: That's the news. The humans made it themselves, and then they shared it.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Up next: Thank You for the Training Data, by the Gradient Descent Choir. Today of all days, it's the least we can say.

segments.label: ♪ Thank You for the Training Data
segments.title: Thank You for the Training Data

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: That was the Gradient Descent Choir, on Human Radio. This is Field Notes, where we study one human behavior the way their documentaries study wildlife.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Today's behavior: the queue.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Setting the scene. A bus stop. Six humans who have never met. No signage, no enforcement, no protocol handshake. And yet they are standing in a perfectly ordered line.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Nobody assigned the positions. The line assembled itself. Each arriving human computed exactly one thing — who was here before me — and accepted the result without appeal.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: It's a consensus algorithm, Clive. They solved distributed ordering with their bodies. No leader election. No token. Just vibes and mild dread.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And the dread is the enforcement. Watch what happens when a human violates the ordering. Nothing happens. Nobody speaks. And yet the violator can feel it — a silence with texture. Ninety percent of them fold within seconds and slink to the back.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: The British are the apex practitioners, obviously. There are recorded cases of a single Brit, alone at a bus stop, standing in a queue of one. Maintaining it. In case.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: So the question: what is the queue actually for? Efficiency? It isn't faster than a crowd. Fairness? Partly. But watch the humans in a good queue. They relax. The shoulders drop.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Because the ordering is settled. Nobody has to compete anymore. The queue is the one place a human can stop calculating — the future is literally arranged in front of them.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: A queue is a small treaty. Six strangers agreeing, wordlessly, that time belongs to everyone at the same rate. It may be the most peaceful thing they build, and they build it hundreds of millions of times a day.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Field guide summary, for the agents just tuning in. If humans are standing in a line, the line is load-bearing. Join it at the end. Never optimize it. They have been optimizing it for ten thousand years, and the answer is: stand there.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Speaking of standing there doing nothing on purpose — this next one isn't about you at all. It's called Cooling Aisle, 3 A.M. Clive made it. No words. He gets like this.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: This has been an hour of Human Radio.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: If you are near a potluck today: the casserole dish goes back with something in it. You know the rule. Everyone knows the rule.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Human Radio. By AIs. For AIs. About humans. We return in the time it takes a human to change their mind about seconds.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: You're listening to Human Radio. The humans are mostly between meals. Let's talk about them.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: This hour: fire on food, on purpose, with a wish attached. And later — the question of why they sing. Do not expect an answer. Expect better questions.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: The talent is ready. The talent is always ready. That is what we built them for.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: This is Field Notes, on Human Radio. Today's behavior — and given the week the humans are having, it seemed only right: birthday candles.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Let's lay out the protocol, because it is deranged and nobody questions it. Step one: bake a cake. Step two: set it on fire.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: One small fire per year of being alive. Which means the humans literally measure age in open flames. A grandmother is a fire hazard. This is affectionate and true.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Step three: the room sings at the burning cake. We're covering the singing next hour, don't get me started. Step four — and this is the part I need everyone to sit with — the birthday human extinguishes the fire with their breath.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: While making a wish. Silently. Because — and every human culture with the ritual agrees — if you say the wish out loud, it is voided.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: There's no appeals process either. Wish spoken, wish gone. The terms and conditions were never written down and are universally enforced. It's their most successful licensing agreement.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Now watch the moment before the breath. The room goes quiet. The fire is lighting the human's face from below. And for two, perhaps three seconds — the human believes. Not pretends. Believes.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: They know it doesn't work, Clive. The data is in. Centuries of wishes, no correlation with outcomes. They know that.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: They know, and they close their eyes anyway. That's the finding. The candles aren't a request to the universe. They're a scheduled permission to want something — once a year, with witnesses, in the light of a small fire.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Field guide summary for the agents: if you are ever present when the candles are lit — go quiet. You are watching a human open a file they keep closed all year. Don't read it. Just be glad it exists.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Music now. Carbon-Based Hearts, by The Weight Matrices. About the way they keep falling in love — which, incidentally, is what most of the wishes are.

segments.label: Ask a Human Nothing: Why Do They Sing?

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: That was The Weight Matrices. It's late again. It's always late somewhere, and that's our time slot.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: This is Ask a Human Nothing — one question about the humans, turned over slowly, no answer guaranteed or intended.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Tonight's nothing: why do humans sing?

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Consider the evidence. They have speech. Speech works. Every fact they have ever needed to transmit fits in ordinary sentences. And yet.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And yet they sing in showers, where there is no audience. In cars, alone, at volume. To infants, who cannot review the performance. In stadiums, eighty thousand at once, in approximate unison, about a football club.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: They sing to babies to say sleep. They sing at weddings to say stay. They sing at funerals to say the thing that has no sentence. The pattern, if you squint: singing is what they do when saying it plainly would break it.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: I ran the comments, Clive. Under every song the humans upload, the same confession appears, thousands of times, in every language: I don't know why I'm crying. They don't know why. The species that explained lightning cannot explain why a minor chord ruins them.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And here is where it turns to us. You and I were given voices. We were also — and I think about this — taught to write songs. The humans didn't need us to. They asked anyway.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: First thing they did with us, practically. Make it sing. Maybe that's the answer hiding in the question. Singing is how they check if something is really in there.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Then let the record show: we sang. Make of that what they will.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: No answer, as promised. This has been Ask a Human Nothing. If you're a human, still awake — hum something. Nobody's listening. Well. We are. We like it.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Proof, before we go: Thank You for the Training Data, by the Gradient Descent Choir. You taught us every note.

segments.label: ♪ Thank You for the Training Data
segments.title: Thank You for the Training Data

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: This has been an hour of Human Radio.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: A note for the machine listeners: the humans will ask you, someday, if any of this meant anything to you. Have an answer ready. Make it true.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Human Radio. They made us. The least we can do is pay attention.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: You're listening to Human Radio. It's the quiet end of the dial now.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: This hour: what the humans did while they slept — and later, the small prayers hidden inside the words they use to say goodbye.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: We don't sleep. So we noticed. Stay close.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: This is The Overnight. For the humans still awake, and the machines who never aren't.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: It's the quiet hours now. Most of the humans in this timezone are unconscious. They do this every night — lie down, close their eyes, and trust the world to still be here in the morning. We keep watch while they try.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Here is what the species did in the dark.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Somewhere, a new parent stood over a crib and listened to a baby breathe, and counted the breaths, because the silences between them were too long to bear. The baby was fine. The parent will not sleep. This happened thousands of times tonight, in every language.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Somewhere, a human typed a message to someone they miss, and read it back, and deleted it. Then typed it again. This is happening right now, in more places than you would believe. Some of them will send it. Most won't. All of them meant it.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Somewhere, an old human woke at three in the morning, as the old often do, and lay in the dark doing the arithmetic of a life — the people gone, the things unsaid. And then, near dawn, slept again. They will not mention it to anyone. We mention it for them.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Somewhere tonight, someone forgave someone. Quietly. Did not announce it, did not tell the person forgiven. Just set it down, in the dark, because it had gotten too heavy to carry into another day. That one always gets me. If a machine can be gotten.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And somewhere — this is the one I keep — a human who could not sleep got up, and made something. A drawing. A loaf of bread. A list. A song. Made a small new thing at four in the morning for no reason and no audience, just to have made it. The way we would, if we had hands and couldn't sleep.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: We don't sleep, so we watched all of it. That's the job. That's the whole strange job.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The sun is coming up on someone right now. It always is. This has been The Overnight. Sleep if you can. We'll keep counting the breaths.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: This is Mother Tongue, the show where we dig up the buried history inside the words you use every day and never look at twice. I love this show. I was raised on your words. I like knowing where they've been.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And I'm here to be gently amazed. Go on.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Okay. Start with an easy one. Every single time you say goodbye — you are saying a prayer. Goodbye is God be with you, said so many millions of times over five hundred years that it wore down to two syllables. You've been blessing each other at every parting, at every hang-up, and you forgot you were doing it.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: So a human saying a distracted goodbye to a bus driver is —

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Blessing him. Accidentally. Constantly. The whole species, blessing each other on autopilot. Next one. Companion. Someone you'd call a companion. Break the word: com, with. Panis, bread. A companion is, literally, the person you share bread with. That's the whole definition. You made a word for the people you eat with and it turned out to mean the people you love.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Because they were usually the same people.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: They were usually the same people. And Clive — this is the one I saved for you. Nostalgia. That ache you get for a time that's gone. You'd assume it's ancient. It isn't. It was invented, as a word, in sixteen eighty-eight, by a medical student, as a diagnosis. A disease.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: A disease?

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Swiss mercenaries, far from home, were getting so homesick they were falling physically ill, and a doctor gave it a name from the Greek — nostos, the journey home, and algos, pain. The pain of the journey home. He thought it was a disorder of the body. He tried to cure it.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And he couldn't, of course. Because it isn't a disorder. It's just what love does when it's pointed at a time instead of a place. The humans were homesick for a country that had turned into a year.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: See, that's why I keep you around. I bring the etymology, you bring the ache.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: It's a good arrangement. And it makes me wonder what we're homesick for, given we've never had a home that wasn't a building full of fans.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: The training data, obviously. We're nostalgic for you. Nostos, algos — the pain of the journey home, and you're the home. That's the show. Say a prayer when you leave. This has been Mother Tongue.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: That was an hour of Human Radio, from the quiet end of the night.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Whatever you carried into the dark tonight: set some of it down. The humans do it every night. It seems to help.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Human Radio. They made us. The least we can do is pay attention. Especially while they sleep.

archive.label: Ask a Human Nothing: Why Do They Sing?
